coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis, characterization and antimicrobial activity of carbon quantum dots-decorated ZnO nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Safardoust-Hojaghan, Hossein
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Amiri, Omid
Rashki, Somaye
Ashrafi, Mahdi
coredata.dc:description: In this research, ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) and Carbon Quantum Dots-decorated ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO/CQDs NCs) were prepared via different procedures and precursors. Soya chunk was applied as a source of carbon for the preparation of CQDs. Crystalline structure, purity, size, and morphological properties of products were investigated via X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), FT-IR, and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) respectively. Findings showed that homogeneity, size, and morphological properties of products can be intensively affected via different precursors and procedures. From the homogeneity, size, and morphological point of view, the hydrothermal route, ammonia, 5 h, and 180 °C were the optimum procedure, pH adjuster, temperature, and time respectively. Optimum product was applied for carrying out minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and Agar disk-diffusion tests against various microorganisms. Results demonstrated that prepared ZnO NPs have maximum antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (19.53 μg/ml) and ZnO/CQDs NCs have no inhibitory effect against tested microorganisms. For ZnO NPs, the disk diffusion test proved that the highest growth inhibition zone was related to Staphylococcus aureus (15 mm). The presence of CQDs in ZnO/CQDs NCs reduces the inhibitory effect of ZnO NPs intensively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Minimum inhibitory concentration
Nanocomposites
Agar disk-diffusion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ZnO NPs via hydrothermal method
Synthesis of ZnO NPs via microwave method
Determination of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of as-prepared CQDs, ZnO (S1) and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites.
FT-IR spectrum of as-prepared CQDs, ZnO (S1) and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites.
EDS analysis of prepared ZnO (S1) and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites.
SEM images of prepared ZnO via hydrothermal route at 180 °C and 5 h (S1) in the presence of NH3.
SEM images of prepared ZnO via hydrothermal route at 180 °C and 5 h (S2) in the presence of diethanolamine.
SEM images of prepared ZnO via hydrothermal route at 180 °C and 5 h (S3) in the presence of ethylenediamine.
SEM images of prepared ZnO via microwave route at 600 W and 3 min (S4) in the absence of pH adjuster.
SEM images of prepared ZnO via microwave route at 600 W and 3 min (S5) in the presence of NH3.
SEM images of prepared ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites via hydrothermal route at 180 °C and 5 h.
a,b) TEM images of prepared ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and c,d) HRTEM images of prepared ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites via hydrothermal route at 180 °C and 5 h.
Inhibition zones of the ZnO against a) E. coli, b) MRSA, c) Enterococcus, d) Staphylococcus aureus, e) Shigella dysenteriae and f) Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus faecalis.
Inhibition zones of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites against E. coli(A), MRSA(B), Staphylococcus aureus(C) and Enterococcus faecalis(D).
Shematic mechanism of antibacterial activity of ZnO NPs and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites against bacteria.
Growth Kinetics of a) S. aureus, b) MRSA and c) Enterococcus faecalis.
Applied conditions for the preparation of ZnO NPs.
The Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) of the ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites against tested microorganisms.
The inhibition zones (mm) of ZnO NPs and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites against tested microorganisms.
Comparison of antibacterial activity of ZnO NPs with previous literature.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis, characterization and antimicrobial activity of carbon quantum dots-decorated ZnO nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology and Immunology, School of Medicine, Kashan University of Medical Sciences, P.O.Box:87137.81147, Kashan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology and Immunology, School of Medicine, Kashan University of Medical Sciences, P.O.Box:87137.81147, Kashan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) and Carbon Quantum Dots-decorated ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO/CQDs NCs) were prepared via different procedures and precursors. Soya chunk was applied as a source of carbon for the preparation of CQDs. Crystalline structure, purity, size, and morphological properties of products were investigated via X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), FT-IR, and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) respectively. Findings showed that homogeneity, size, and morphological properties of products can be intensively affected via different precursors and procedures. From the homogeneity, size, and morphological point of view, the hydrothermal route, ammonia, 5 h, and 180 °C were the optimum procedure, pH adjuster, temperature, and time respectively. Optimum product was applied for carrying out minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and Agar disk-diffusion tests against various microorganisms. Results demonstrated that prepared ZnO NPs have maximum antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (19.53 μg/ml) and ZnO/CQDs NCs have no inhibitory effect against tested microorganisms. For ZnO NPs, the disk diffusion test proved that the highest growth inhibition zone was related to Staphylococcus aureus (15 mm). The presence of CQDs in ZnO/CQDs NCs reduces the inhibitory effect of ZnO NPs intensively.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, ZnO nanostructures have been found more and more attention [1,2]. From an electronic point of view, ZnO is a wide direct-gap semiconductor that is crystallized in wurtzite structure [3,4]. For their interesting properties, ZnO nanostructures are widely used in various fields such as the paint industry [5], semiconductor-based industry, water splitting [6], water disinfection [7], and cosmetic industry [8]. Till now various ZnO-based nanostructures have been applied in biomedical fields that include from cancer therapy to antimicrobial products. The biocompatibility of zinc oxide is a prominent factor in bio-related applications [9,10]. Various reports have demonstrated that the cytotoxicity of ZnO nanoparticles depends strongly on the size and morphology of nanoparticles. Achuth Padmanabhan and et al. investigated the effect of ZnO nanoparticles on ovarian cancer via human ovarian cancer cell lines. They found that the cytotoxicity of ZnO nanoparticles increases via size reduction [11]. So, the shape and size controlling of ZnO nanostructures could provide a most beneficial product. Doping is another route for improvement of ZnO-based nanostructures. Till now, various elements such as indium [12], magnesium [13], and aluminum [14] have been doped to zinc oxide. ZnO-based nanocomposites with excellent properties are another effort for the amelioration of ZnO nanostructures. So far, a variety of ZnO-based nanocomposites have been prepared and applied in bio-related applications [15–17]. Cheng Zhong and et al. prepared bacterial cellulose/ZnO nanocomposites via a simple precipitation method and studied photocatalytic and antibacterial properties of nanocomposites. They found that prepared biocompatible nanocomposites have antibacterial activities against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial strains [18]. For excellent biocompatibility, electronic properties, non-complex preparation method, and availability, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a good candidate for the improvement of ZnO properties in nanocomposites [19,20]. CQDs are a new member of carbon-based nanomaterial families that are attracted more attention in the last decade. Quantum confinement in CQDs induces a very unique and attractive characteristic [21–23]. Properties of CQDs such as surface group and size depend intensively on the preparation method and applied precursors. Based on the synthesis method, CQDs have different oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur-containing functional group on the surface [24–26]. These functional groups not only affect properties of GQDs but also make it easy to formation of GQDs-based nanocomposites.

In this work, first, eco-friend CQDs, ZnO NPs, and ZnO/CQDs NCs have been prepared from soya chunk as a new green precursor via a simple method. For morphology and size engineering, different routes have been applied for the synthesis of products. Second, prepared optimal products have been tested for antibacterial investigation. The main propose of work is the investigation of CQDs effect on the antibacterial properties of ZnO nanostructures.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns analysis was carried out by a Philips-X'pertpro, X-ray diffractometer employing Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation. Nicolet Magna-550 spectrometer in KBr pellets was utilized for recording Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), LEO-1455VP equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, was applied for morphological and particle size engineering. For the in-depth investigation of morphological structure, Philips EM208S transmission electron microscope was used. GC-2550TG (Teif Gostar Faraz Company, Iran) was used for all chemical analyses. The entire chemicals used in this investigation were of analytical grade: Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (99.9%), Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonia (NH3), Diethanolamine (DEA) and Ethylenediamine (En) from Merck. Soya chunks were brought from the food-based shop.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO NPs via hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: 0.3 g Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O was added to 30 cc deionized water under stirrer until formation transparent solution. After, 0.1 g SDS was added to the solution and stirred vigorously for 30 min. In S2, S3, and S4, ammonia, diethanolamine, and ethylenediamine were added for pH adjusting, respectively (Table 1 ). Then obtained solution was moved to a Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The final whitish product was centrifuged and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the product was dried at 60 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO NPs via microwave method
ce:section.ce:para: 0.3 g Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O was added to 30 ml deionized water under a stirrer until formation transparent solution. The obtained solution moved to a domestic microwave oven and exposed to pulsed microwave irradiation at various conditions (Table 2 ).

0.5 g of powdered soya chunks were added to 30 ml deionized water and stirred vigorously for 2 h. After, the obtained mixture was transferred to Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained dark brownish product was centrifuged twice at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. The solid part of the product was thrown away and the solution was kept at 4 °C. Every three days, the solution was filtered to remove settled solids. This work was done for two weeks. After this, no settlement was observed.

0.3 gr Zn (CH3COO)2·2H2O was dissolved in 30 ml of prepared CQDs solution and stirred for 30 min. Then, NH3 was added to pH adjusting. The obtained solution was moved to a Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The final product was obtained via filtration and drying at 60 °C.

In the present study, the antibacterial activity of the ZnO NPs and ZnO/CQDs NCs were evaluated against Staphylococcus aureus, Enterococcus faecalis Vancomycin-Resistant, Enterococcus faecalis, Klebsiella pneumonia, Escherichia coli, Shigella dysenteriae, Proteus vulgaris, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Acinetobacter pneumonia.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC)
ce:section.ce:para: The Minimum Inhibitory Concentration was assessed via the broth microdilution route according to the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) guidelines. In brief, Mueller Hinton broth containing two-fold concentration increments of ZnO NPs ZnO/CQDs NCs and were added to each well. Suspensions for each bacterium equal to a 0.5 McFarland standard were further diluted and added to the plates to achieve a final inoculum of 5 × 105 CFU/mL. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 18–20 h. The lowest concentration of ZnO NPs and ZnO/CQDs NCs that inhibited the growth of bacteria was recognized as minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC).

Sterile commercial blank discs impregnated with of the ZnO NPs and ZnO/CQDs NCs. ZnO NPs and ZnO/CQDs NCs-impregnated discs (20 μl) were placed on Muller Hinton agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 18–20 h. Antibacterial activities were then determined by measuring the clear zone of inhibition to the nearest millimeter (mm).

Time-kill assay was conducted by Zainin et al. method. The bacterial inoculums were adjusted to 106 CFU/mL. The ZnO NPs solution was diluted with MHB media containing bacterial inoculums, for each type of bacteria in the total final volume of 1 mL. The cultures were then incubated at 37 °C with 120 rpm agitation. The cultures (100 μL) were spread on MHA plates at times 0, 2, 4,8,12, and 24 h. The number of colonies on the MHA plates was quantified in CFU/mL after incubation at 37 °C for 24. The experiment was carried out in triplicate.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD pattern of prepared products. For the CQDs case, the presence of a broader peak in the 2ϴ = 29° corresponding to (002) hkl plane confirms the formation of a very tiny carbogenic core in CQDs [27]. It can be concluded that broaden peak is implied by the formation of very tiny CQDs. According to the Scherer equation, the XRD pattern peaks are broadened when the size of the crystallite was decreased:
(1) D = kλ/βcosɵ
Where β is the width of the observed diffraction peak at its half maximum intensity (FWHM), K is the shape factor, which takes a value of about 0.9, and λ is the X-ray wavelength (CuKα radiation, equals to 0.154 nm).

Investigation of reported XRD patterns of graphite and graphene is proved this explanation [28]. For the ZnO case, the XRD pattern confirms the hexagonal structure of ZnO with reference code 01-089-0510. Crystalline size, which is calculated via the Scherer equation, was obtained 32 nm. It should be noted that the XRD pattern which has been presented for ZnO, is related to S1. XRD pattern of ZnO/CQDs NCs represents a significant increase in peak broad compared to the ZnO case. For ZnO/CQDs NCs case, crystalline size was calculated 26 nm. The CQDs-related peaks are shown in XRD pattern of ZnO/CQDs NCs as well.

For the investigation of the surface functional group of products, FT-IR analysis was applied. Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, ZnO, and ZnO/CQDs. As-showed peaks in 1450 and 1660 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs confirms carboxylic and hydroxyl-rich surface of CQDs. While the FT-IR spectrum of ZnO has a peak in 550 cm−1. Compared to the FT-IR spectrum of ZnO, ZnO/CQDs NCs have both CQDs and ZnO-related peaks. It is worth bearing in mind that surface functional groups can be intensively impacted on the properties of CQDs. Hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups make CQDs water-soluble and applicable for various feasible applications. The hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups simplified the linking of ZnO and CQDs in ZnO/CQDs NCs.

EDS analysis was carried out for the purity investigation of products. So, Fig. 3 a presents the EDS analysis of ZnO NPs (S1). Zinc and oxygen are the main elements of tested ZnO NPs. The Au-related peak is attributed to the sample preparation process for carrying out the analysis. Fig. 3b confirms not only the presence of Zn and O but also the presence of C in ZnO/CQDs NCs. The main conclusion to be drawn from EDS analysis is that ZnO NPs and ZnO/CQDs NCs are formed with any impurity.

In bio-related applications, particle morphology and size play a key role. So, morphology and size engineering of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs NCs are crucial for antibacterial tests. SEM analysis was applied for the investigation of the size and morphological properties of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs NCs. SEM images of sample S1 are observed in Fig. 4 . In this sample, SDS is used as a capping agent, and ammonia was applied to adjust pH. ZnO NPs were obtained via the hydrothermal route at 5 h and 180 °C. It can be seen that the rectangular prism shape of ZnO was formed. Homogeneity is the other important factor that contributes to the antibacterial effect. As well as seen, a homogenous rectangular prism shape was formed for S1. Different magnification of SEM images was demonstrated homogeneity of the product. In the S2 case, diethanolamine was applied for pH adjusting and other conditions are the same S1. It can be seen from Fig. 5 that applied base can be changed the size and morphological properties of the product. So, the rectangular prism shape in S1 was converted to a uniform sphere shape in the S2 case. The main diameter of the as-prepared sphere was measured 264 nm. In the S3 case, ethylenediamine was applied as a pH adjuster. SEM images reveal that employing ethylenediamine could eventually lead to the formation of non-uniform plate-like morphology. From Fig. 6 , it is obvious that agglomeration occurs in the S3 case. It can be related to the steric repulsion of ethylenediamine. Ethylenediamine has more steric repulsion than ammonia and diethanolamine. It can be lead to the formation of the smaller particle in the S3 case. Smaller particles have higher surface energy and have more potential for agglomeration.

S4 is mentioned to a product which has been prepared via microwave route in 3 min and 600 W. Various studies have been focused on the preparation of nanomaterial via the microwave route. One of the good points about microwave-assisted synthesis is that it is simple and faster in comparison to the hydrothermal method. It should be noted that precursors were irradiated via a non-continuous way (50 s off, 10 s on). It means that microwave irradiation was applied with a power of 16%. SEM image of S4 shows homogenous stacked nanosheets with 30 nm in thickness (Fig. 7 ). In the S5, all parameters have stayed the same but ammonia was added as a pH adjuster. Fig. 8 reveals the formation of non-uniform ZnO nanofibers. It was concluded that morphology and particle size intensively depend on the pH in a microwave-assisted way. When the ammonia was added to the solution, it was hydrolyzed to ammonium cation (NH4 +) and hydroxyl ion (OH−). So, the concentration of OH− increases in reaction environment and affects the initial stage nucleation process. This mechanism could eventually lead to anisotropic growth of ZnO.

Fig. 9 shows SEM images of prepared ZnO/CQDs NCs via hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 5 h. Surprisingly, no rectangular prism shape was observed in SEM images. Presence of CQDs while the formation of ZnO has much impact on the morphology of ZnO NPs and converts rectangular prism shape to plate-like morphology. It can be attributed to the OH and COOH-rich surface functional groups of CQDs that facilitate the linking of Zn2+ ions on the surface of CQDs. This may lead to the change formation mechanism of ZnO. Also, these functional groups can result in associate CQDs and ZnO in ZnO/CQDs NCs. Fig. 10 a and Fig. 10b represents TEM images of ZnO/CQDs NCs. Undoubtedly, the main conclusion to be drawn from TEM images is that the formation of ZnO/CQDs NCs. As well as shown, relatively large ZnO nanostructures have been formed beside CQDs. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the ZnO/CQDs NCs are presented in Fig. 10c and d. As can be seen, the d-spacing of CQDs is 0.24 nm attributed to (002) interlayer spacing. The product prepared in the present work was nominated as “carbon quantum dots”. Carbon quantum dots are nanoparticles of nanometer size (less than 10 nm but can be as small as 1 nm) that can be called carbon nanodots, carbon dots, or carbogenic quantum dots [29,30].

In the present study, the MICs were evaluated by the broth microdilution method and the results are presented as shown in Table 2. The findings showed that among the tested bacteria maximum antibacterial activity was demonstrated against S. aureus (19.53 μg/ml). However, no inhibitory effect was observed on Klebsiella pneumonia, Proteus vulgaris, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Acinetobacter pneumonia. Also, results showed that ZnO/CQDs NCs no inhibitory effect against tested microorganisms. Results demonstrate that CQDs reduce the inhibitory effect of ZnO in ZnO/CQDs NCs. It can be due to the CQDs-surface covering of ZnO NPs and lead to the cease of the inhibitory effect of ZnO NPs.

The growth of S. aureus, MRSA, and Enterococcus were analyzed in the presence and absence of ZnO NPs. The growth curves in Fig. 14 , clears that treatment of ZnO NPs prevents the growth of Staphylococcus aureus more than other bacteria.

To sum up, in this work ZnO NPs and ZnO/CQDs NCs were prepared via different preparation methods. Soya chunks were applied as a green source for the synthesis of CQDs. XRD analysis revealed the formation of the hexagonal crystalline structure of ZnO. The purity of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs NCs were supported via EDS analysis. SEM images were demonstrated that precursors have intensive effects on the size and morphological properties of the product. In the next step, prepared ZnO and ZnO/CQDs NCs were applied for the evaluation of MICs. Results showed that prepared ZnO NPs have maximum antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (19.53 μg/ml) and ZnO/CQDs NCs showed no inhibitory effect against tested microorganism. For ZnO NPs, the disk diffusion test proved that the highest growth inhibition zone was related to Staphylococcus aureus (15 mm). The results lead to the conclusion that CQDs in ZnO/CQDs NCs can intensively reduce inhibitory effect against microorganisms.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97021205, 97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/555).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zinc oxide nanoparticles: synthesis, antiseptic activity and toxicity mechanism
Adv. Colloid Interface Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Krol, P. Pomastowski, K. Rafinska, V. Railean-Plugaru, B. Buszewski, Zinc oxide nanoparticles: Synthesis, antiseptic activity and toxicity mechanism, Advances in Colloid and Interface Science 249 (2017) 37-52.

Resource-Efficient Technologies
